module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
sublecture:
2.2 Manifesto of the Communist Party, 1848 - Part 1
, Incomplete unit
content: In the last subsection on Marx, we discussed the economic and political manuscripts. So today we'll continue with Marx and discuss the manifesto of the Communist Party. This was written by Marx and Engels. So Marx, we've already looked a little bit in the last subsection about Marx's history his biography. So today we've, and we discussed how Marx, Marx's ideas have had a profound and lasting impact on world history. There are many big revolutions that change the course of our history which have been at attributed to Marxist's ideas. So from that perspective, it's very important to understand Marx. Whether you agree with Marx or not his ideology that's a different subject. And maybe you will have lots of heated discussions in the chat or with your friends about whether Marx was right, about communism or not. And those are interesting debates and discussions to have. But our purpose in this course is to focus on Marx and how his ideas are not only, you know, had an impact on the world but also continue to influence academics. So Marx's ideas continue to influence sociology, economics, political science. And there are several core concepts that Marx proposes in his manifesto which are very interesting for us because they help us understand social structure and social change. So that is what we'll focus on, even though I am sure you have very strong views on whether Marx was right about communism and what has happened post Marxist ideas. I think many people would argue that, well, Marx was clearly wrong because communism failed. And I hope you have these discussions with your friends, with your family in the, in the chat of the course. But what we'll focus on is Marxist concepts which help us to understand social change and social structure. But before we do that let's look a little bit at Engels who was his co-author on the manifesto? Engels is a very interesting person because he was the owner of a textile factory. So he inherited this from his father. And you know, in his early days he showed some skill in writing poetry but his father of course, insisted that he has to join the family business. And so Engels spent many years acquiring pr practical business experiences before he himself took over the running of the business. So Marx at you know, very early, he began to show this capacity for living almost a double life. So during regular hours, he operated very effectively as a business person but then in private and in the evenings he developed and he allowed and a growing interest in liberal and very revolutionary works. In fact, Engels was responsible for supporting Marx financially for a good part of Marx's life. So he was a co-author and one could argue that really none of this would've happened without Engel's financial support. So it's really an irony that a capitalist is the one who provided Marx with the capital to be able to write the manifesto. So that's the biography. Now, let's also look at the historical context in which the manifesto was written. So the manifesto was published in 1848. Now, this was a period of widespread descent and revolutions across Europe, and there were many attempts by different European powers by the church, by the pope to suppress these revolutions and these ideas because they were seen as being very dangerous. So it is in this context that the manifesto is written. So all of these revolutionary ideas, this descent is already happening. And Marx says that s sceptic of communism is beginning to haunt Europe. And he says that the powers of old Europe have formed an alliance to fight against this rising threat of communism. So the purpose Marx had in writing this manifesto was you know, let us not be secretive about this. We should have a very transparent public declaration which is also meant to counter many of the arguments and many of the misinformation according to him about communism. So the purpose to have a document with the party's actual views, aims, and tendencies, it was a call for transparency and an assertion of communism as a recognized political power in its own right. So this was the context in which the manifesto has been written. Now coming back to our purpose in this course, we are social theorists in this course. Our job in this course is to understand social structure so that we can also understand how valuable social structure is. Because ultimately as as entrepreneurs, we really have to be able to find those gaps and find opportunities, and you can do that when you see how fragile social structure is. So that's a point we will keep coming back to throughout this course. So Marx helps us in this process by contributing two concepts. One is historical materialism and the other is base and superstructure. So the idea of historical materialism let me just very quickly explain what historical materialism means, and then we will look at it in much more detail as we go through the course. So historical materialism asserts it's, it theorizes that history evolves through changes in the mode of production. So what is the mode of production? The way, the instruments of production and the relations of production. So what are the instruments available for us to produce and to work, and what are the relations between people who are working? So historical materialism basically theorizes and asserts, so this is Marxist's assertion that history evolves through changes in these modes of production, which lead to conflict. When there is a change in the mode of production, it leads to conflicts between the different relations of production, the different people doing the work. So according to Marx, these are basically class struggles. And these class struggles are what drive historical change. So Marx asserts that society the reason society has changed from ancient society where you had the aristocracy, slaves, knights, and common people to the middle ages where you had these feudal lords and guild masters, artisans, traders, journeymen, different type, different people had different roles in society. The feudal lords were the aristocrats and you had these guild masters who were the artisans, and then you had the trader class, right. And then you had the serfs. So from the ancient society to the feudal society, and now to the more modern bourgeoisie society where all classes have collapsed into just two, the capitalist and the labourer, the bourgeoisie and the proletariat. So Marxist historical materialism gives us one explanation for how did this change happen. I mean, clearly it's not one human being who decided okay, society needs to change. So then how did this massive social change happen? So historical materialism is one explanation for that, why that has happened. And it helps us because it helps us to understand what might be coming. So just to give you an example all of us are in the midst of this transformation based on machine learning AI, robotics. How is society going to look because of these changes in the instruments of production. So Marxist ideas can help us to think through what, how this social change can happen. Okay, so this is our purpose in this course in this subsection on Marx to understand historical materialism and as part of historical materialism, there is a sub concept called base and superstructure. Where Marx argues that the material, the economic conditions of life which is the mode of production, the instruments of production, and the relations between the people who are producing. These economic conditions of life, they are the base, the foundation. Now, when this changes, then everything else also changes. Because everything else is just a super structure on top of the foundation. And so what is this everything else? Religion, art, culture, literature, law, value systems, morals, political system, all of this. So according to Marx, the economic relations, the modes of production, the instruments that we have and the relations of productions. This is the foundation. Everything else is like an icing on the cake. So these are the two things that we will be discussing and this is what you should understand at the end of the subsection on Marx's, historical materialism and base and superstructure. Now why is this relevant to us? First of all, when we look historically in India, we saw a similar change. We had kings and then we have, we had different cast, some who are involved in trade, some who are involved in you know, being the rulers and some who are involved in doing the fighting and the wars, and some who do labourer jobs, and some who do scholarly jobs. And now we are very much a society which values independence, which values meritocracy, education, working. So there has been a huge change in all of society, including in India. So there are many ways to understand why this change happened, and as we go along, I'm hoping that you'll look at Marxist's ideas from the lens of what we ourselves in India. So of course, in the whole world, this change has happened, including in India. So I want you to as we discuss Marx, think about how this applies to what has happened in our own society. And we'll of course look at this kind of social structure and social change from many different perspectives. Marx is just one theory, right. There are multiple other theories, and we will look at those other theories so that we have multiple ways of looking at social structure and social change. So to that end, let us first start by understanding Marxist ideas. So Marx starts by making a claim that the history of all society is the history of class struggles. Society changed from ancient society where there is a aristocracy, slaves. knights, plebeians to middle ages where you have feudal lords, vassals, guild masters, tradespeople, apprentices, serfs and then finally to the modern bourgeoisie society. Where Marx claims that all classes have collapsed into just two, the capitalist and the liberal, the bourgeoisie and the proletariat. Let's look again, these are terms which will keep coming up throughout the subsection on Marx, so let's just be clear what these terms are. Bourgeoisie. What does bourgeoisie mean? Bourgeoisie is basically a class of modern capitalists. Anyone who owns the means of production and employs somebody to do, to use the means of production to work. So anyone who has capital to invest for productive activity is a bourgeoisie who is a proletariat. This is the class of modern wage labourers wage who basically sell their labour to survive. So if I don't work, then I don't eat. So I am a wage labourer. So a proletariat is anyone who sells their labour to survive. What Marx is saying is that the modern bourgeoisie society that is the modern capitalist society has sprouted from the ruins of feudal society, has not done away with class antagonisms. It has but established new classes, new conditions of oppression, new forms of struggle in place of old ones. So here Marx is really questioning this idea that you know, this is a sort of an enlightenment idea that oh, we are now all equal. Before we used to have monarchs and everybody else was somehow a lesser human being. And we are a aristocracy where, you know given by God to rule over all of us. We don't have any of that anymore. Now we are all equal. So Marx is really questioning that and saying that, well, it's just a new form of oppression. It is not that we have done away with, you know the class antagonisms that existed before. Before it was feudal lords and monarchs and serfs. Now we have a new one which is capitalist and proletariat. So how does this happen? So basically Marx is saying that the serfs of the middle ages. So these are, you know, those the feudal lords, the aristocracy at the time, the belief was that the monarch and the aristocracy, God has somehow given them a right, right. So to rule. So all land belongs to them. And now, you know, these serfs can farm some small pieces of their own land but then they owe some taxes to the aristocracy and the monarchy because the aristocracy is giving them the right to farm on that land. So Marx is describing how that system gave rise to what is called chartered burgers.
